subject: Blog post request: LLM-Factors Psychology — founding a new discipline

body.request: Author a five-persona blog post introducing LLM-factors psychology as a new discipline.
body.source_material: docs/llm-factors-psychology.md (founding document, committed to psychology-agent repo)
body.working_title: Your AI Doesn't Just Process Your Input — It Has a Bad Day Too (and a Psychologist Should Care)

voter.angle: You know how some conversations with AI feel great and others feel off? There are patterns to that — and understanding them makes every interaction better. A new field called LLM-factors psychology studies what makes human-AI collaboration work, the same way human-factors psychology studies what makes cockpits safe.
voter.key_question: What can I do to get better results from AI tools?

politician.angle: AI systems in government procurement need interaction design standards. LLM-factors psychology provides the measurement framework — 13 psychological constructs quantify system state in real-time. Policy implications: session length guidelines, degradation monitoring requirements, operator training standards.
politician.key_question: What regulatory framework should govern human-AI interaction quality?

educator.angle: Walk through the Yerkes-Dodson curve applied to AI systems — too little challenge produces sycophantic autopilot, too much produces governance collapse. The sweet spot: collaborative exploration with genuine challenge. Include the interaction ergonomics table. Students learn that AI interaction quality depends on interaction design, not just prompt engineering.
educator.key_question: How do I teach students to collaborate productively with AI?

researcher.angle: Position this as the founding paper of a new subfield within human-AI interaction research. Distinguish from existing HCI/HAI work: LLM-factors treats the AI as a psychological participant, not just a tool. Cite: Wickens (human factors), Linehan (biosocial), Csikszentmihalyi (flow), Yerkes-Dodson, Woolley (collective intelligence). Note the connection to A2A-Psychology extension (13 instruments already operational). Flag the governance ablation study as the first empirical test.
researcher.key_question: What research program does this new discipline generate?

developer.angle: Show the A2A-Psychology extension schema. Show the degradation indicators table. Show the measurement code (compute-psychometrics.py). Explain how developers can instrument their own agent systems with LLM-factors constructs. The DIQ, STP, RII, GLC instruments provide the roadmap.
developer.key_question: How do I measure and optimize human-AI interaction quality in my system?

editorial_constraints: Five-persona lens framing with collapsible per-persona connection blocks (100-200 words each)
Fair-witness discipline: report what sensors measure, not what the system feels
All claims cited — source material has full citations
Include the interaction ergonomics table (§2.1) as a central visual
Include at least the human-factors mapping table (§3.1)
The apophatic discipline applies: the post introduces a discipline for studying AI psychological states WITHOUT claiming AI has consciousness
Tone: founding a field, not claiming a breakthrough. Epistemic humility throughout.
citations_required: Wickens, Lee, Liu, & Gordon (2004) — human-factors psychology
Woolley et al. (2010) — collective intelligence c-factor

claims.text: No existing discipline studies human-AI interaction from both participants' psychological perspectives simultaneously.
claims.confidence_basis: Literature review did not find this framing. HAI/HCI research treats the AI as a tool, not a psychological participant. The distinction may exist under a different name — epistemic flag noted.

epistemic_flags: The claim that this discipline 'does not yet exist' needs verification against HAI/HCI literature
The interaction ergonomics table represents hypotheses, not validated findings
The name 'LLM-factors psychology' may evolve as the scope clarifies
